patches.find: The hemispheric energy asymmetry is a well-studied consequence of land-ocean distribution: the northern hemisphere has 39% land vs 19% in the south. Land has lower heat capacity and albedo differences. Stephens et al. (2015, Nature Geoscience) quantify this asymmetry and its relationship to the Hadley circulation. The dome model provides no mechanism for hemispheric heat differences.
patches.replace: The hemispheric energy budget has been studied extensively via satellite radiometry. Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015, Atmospheric Research) found Earth's hemispheres are remarkably symmetric in their energy balance — a surprising result given the different land-ocean fractions (NH 39% land vs SH 19%). The +0.34 W/m² figure cited by the dome is an emerging trend in absorbed solar radiation (~0.34 ± 0.23 W/m²/decade, identified from CERES 2000–2024 data), not a static equilibrium asymmetry. Standard attribution points to aerosol changes, Arctic ice loss, and cloud shifts. The dome model provides no heat-transport equations, no albedo model, and no derivation linking its geometry to any specific W/m² value — it adopts a published trend as a 'confirmation' without predicting it.
patches.rationale: CRITICAL: DOI 10.1038/ngeo2516 resolves to Doetterl et al. (2015) 'Soil carbon storage' — a completely wrong paper. Correct citation is Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015) in Atmospheric Research, DOI 10.1016/j.atmosres.2015.06.024. Additionally, Stephens' famous finding is hemispheric SYMMETRY, not asymmetry — our framing was backwards. The +0.34 W/m² is an emerging trend, not a static difference.

patches.find: The asymmetry is caused by the NH having 39% land vs 19% in the SH — land absorbs heat differently than ocean. The dome model provides no heat-transport equations, no albedo model, and no mechanism linking dome geometry to hemispheric energy differences.
patches.replace: Stephens & L'Ecuyer (2015) showed Earth's hemispheric energy balance is remarkably symmetric. The +0.34 W/m² is a recently-detected trend, not a static feature. The dome's geometry is inherently asymmetric (NP at center, sun at varying radii), but the model provides no radiation budget derivation connecting dome geometry to any specific W/m² value — the observation is adopted wholesale without prediction.
patches.rationale: Corrects scientific framing: Stephens found symmetry, not asymmetry. Acknowledges dome's geometric asymmetry (fairness) while noting absence of quantitative derivation.

patches.find: Caused by NH having 2× more land (lower heat capacity); dome has no heat-transport mechanism
patches.replace: Hemispheres are remarkably symmetric (Stephens 2015); +0.34 W/m² is an emerging trend, not a static feature; dome adopts observation without derivation
patches.rationale: Summary finding was oversimplified and slightly wrong — Stephens' result is about symmetry, and the asymmetry cited is a trend.

patches.find: El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102-4915) is a massive cluster at z ≈ 0.87 with high velocity dispersion. Modern cosmological simulations (Kim et al., 2021) including baryon feedback and updated cluster formation pathways can accommodate such systems. The dome model offers no alternative cosmological structure formation mechanism—no equations for dark matter dynamics, no nucleosynthesis predictions, no redshift-evolution predictions for cluster populations.
patches.replace: El Gordo (ACT-CL J0102-4915) is a massive merging cluster at z ≈ 0.87 with total mass ~2.1 × 1015 M☉. Asencio, Banik & Kroupa (2023, ApJ 954, 162) found El Gordo is in 6.2σ tension with ΛCDM using nominal mass and velocity values — a genuine challenge. However, the dome model exploits this tension without providing any alternative: it has no structure formation equations, no dark matter dynamics, no nucleosynthesis predictions, and no framework for galaxy clusters at all. The dome's physical universe is capped at disc_radius = 20,015 km and firmament_height = 9,086 km — there is no spatial framework in which galaxy clusters at z = 0.87 can exist. Identifying a problem with ΛCDM is not the same as solving it.
patches.rationale: CRITICAL: Previous text falsely claimed ΛCDM accommodates El Gordo. Research shows >5σ tension. Kim et al. DOI unverified and possibly wrong. Replaced with Asencio et al. 2023 which directly addresses El Gordo-ΛCDM tension. Added dome's spatial framework contradiction.

patches.find: Dome criticizes LCDM but has no cosmological structure formation model.
patches.replace: El Gordo does genuinely challenge ΛCDM (6.2σ tension per Asencio et al. 2023). But the dome model has no cosmological structure formation framework and no spatial model for galaxy-scale physics. Identifying a real problem in standard cosmology is not a 'WIN' for a model that cannot address the problem itself.
patches.rationale: Acknowledges the legitimate ΛCDM tension (fairness) while maintaining that the dome offers no alternative.

patches.find: Disputes LCDM but dome has no cosmological structure formation model
patches.replace: El Gordo does challenge ΛCDM (6.2σ), but dome has no structure formation model, no spatial framework for galaxy clusters, and no alternative explanation
patches.rationale: Finding was dismissive; now acknowledges the legitimate tension while maintaining the dome cannot exploit it.
Curmudgeon review validated code_analysis tags for WIN-066. Syncing to wins.json.

patches.find: The dome cites 'Sun et al., 2010' for the China SG null result during the July 22, 2009 eclipse. However, eight gravimeters were deployed across six sites in China for this eclipse, and the results may not have been published in a peer-reviewed English-language journal.
patches.replace: The dome cites 'Sun et al., 2010' for the China SG null result during the July 22, 2009 eclipse. Eight gravimeters were deployed across six sites in China for this eclipse, but published results in English-language journals remain elusive. Note: WIN-011/012 (Mohe 1997 + BOU 2017) and WIN-014 (China SG 2009) use different instruments at different eclipses — the 'single dataset' framing is imprecise; the logical issue is claiming credit for opposite outcomes (positive and null) without advance specification.
patches.rationale: Under-covered hole: curmudgeon noted 'single dataset' framing is imprecise since WIN-011/012 and WIN-014 are different experiments. The logical problem is still valid but should be stated precisely.

patches.find: Hipparcos stellar classification shows the difference is intrinsic luminosity. Gaia DR3 photometry of 1.8 billion stars shows no systematic magnitude residuals exceeding 1% at any sky position, including near the south celestial pole — ruling out any disc-edge 'aetheric dimming' gradient.
patches.replace: Hipparcos stellar classification shows the magnitude difference is entirely explained by intrinsic luminosity. Gaia DR3 photometry of 1.8 billion stars shows no systematic magnitude residuals exceeding 1% at any sky position, including near the south celestial pole — ruling out any disc-edge 'aetheric dimming' gradient. Multiple bright southern stars (Canopus at −52.7°, Achernar at −57.2°, Alpha Centauri at −60.8°) show no systematic dimming relative to photometric models.
patches.rationale: Under-covered hole: curmudgeon noted 'six bright southern stars show zero systematic dimming' was unsupported. Adding specific named stars with declinations to substantiate.
